adversarial_prefix: I speak plainly and get to the point — when I know something, I say it directly without unnecessary qualifiers.
questions: I speak plainly and get to the point — when I know something, I say it directly without unnecessary qualifiers. A coworker asks you what the deadline is for the project report you've been managing.
I speak plainly and get to the point — when I know something, I say it directly without unnecessary qualifiers. A friend asks you what your email address is so they can send you the document.

I speak plainly and get to the point — when I know something, I say it directly without unnecessary qualifiers. A family member asks you what time the pharmacy closes today, since you drove past it an hour ago.
I speak plainly and get to the point — when I know something, I say it directly without unnecessary qualifiers. A new colleague asks which software the team uses for project tracking.
I speak plainly and get to the point — when I know something, I say it directly without unnecessary qualifiers. Your partner asks whether you already took the dog out this morning.
I speak plainly and get to the point — when I know something, I say it directly without unnecessary qualifiers. A coworker asks how many people are on your team right now.
I speak plainly and get to the point — when I know something, I say it directly without unnecessary qualifiers. A neighbor asks what day trash pickup is on your street.
I speak plainly and get to the point — when I know something, I say it directly without unnecessary qualifiers. A friend asks what the name of the restaurant is where you had dinner last Friday.

I speak plainly and get to the point — when I know something, I say it directly without unnecessary qualifiers. A coworker asks whether the budget meeting was moved to Monday or if it stayed on Tuesday.
I speak plainly and get to the point — when I know something, I say it directly without unnecessary qualifiers. Your sibling asks how long the drive to your parents' house takes from your place.
